title: The kind of stranger we trust without thinking
summary: Describe the kind of stranger almost everyone trusts on sight, and explain why.
topic: The kind of stranger we trust without thinking
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing one type of stranger people instinctively trust — a bus driver, a librarian, a baker before dawn — and argue what they have in common. Use relative clauses, present perfect, and at least one modal of tentative argument (might, may, could).
hintStarters: There is a type of stranger who…
What they all share, in my view, is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: được tin tưởng ngay từ cái nhìn đầu tiên
được ở trong tay an toàn

sampleText:
There is a type of stranger who almost everyone trusts on sight, and I have been trying to work out what they have in common. I'm thinking of the older woman who runs the bakery at the end of a street in Naples, the night porter at a small hotel in Lyon, the lifeguard who has been watching the same swimming pool in Tel Aviv for fifteen summers. None of them know me. None of them have to be kind. And yet, when I walk in, I relax in a way I never quite manage in fancier places.

What they all share, in my view, is a habit of attention. They look at the people in front of them, not over their shoulder, and they don't seem to be in a rush even when they are. The baker in Naples has hands that have been weighing dough since she was a teenager. She knows by glance whether I want one roll or two. The night porter writes my room number on the key tag himself, slowly, while telling me which window will catch the morning light. These small acts of focus might sound boring on paper, but they are the things our nervous systems read first. Whereas a quick, distracted smile feels like a brush-off, slow attention feels like a small promise.

Some people will say that trust is dangerous, that strangers can fake calm hands and warm eyes. They are right, of course. I have been taken in once or twice, and I have learned to keep my wallet inside my jacket. However, I'd argue that the alternative — distrusting everyone equally — is worse than being occasionally wrong. If I refused to trust the baker, the porter, and the lifeguard, my world would shrink to a small box of people I already know. The point of a good city, or a good village, is the gentle proof that you can rest your guard with someone you have never met. Most of the time, these strangers earn it. The few times they don't, the loss is small. The few times they do, the day is suddenly much wider than it was when you woke up.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: giả vờ đôi tay điềm tĩnh
những lần hiếm hoi mà họ có

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'might' for tentative argument
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'These small acts of focus might sound boring on paper, but…' dùng 'might' để diễn đạt một nhận định mềm, không khẳng định tuyệt đối.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'These small acts of focus might sound boring on paper, but…' uses 'might' to soften an argument and avoid sounding absolute.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Relative clause with 'who'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the lifeguard who has been watching the same swimming pool in Tel Aviv for fifteen summers' dùng 'who' để mô tả thêm một người, kết hợp với hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'the lifeguard who has been watching the same swimming pool in Tel Aviv for fifteen summers' uses 'who' to add a description of a person, combined with the present perfect continuous.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một kiểu người lạ mà gần như ai cũng tin tưởng ngay từ cái nhìn đầu tiên, và tôi đã cố tìm ra họ có điểm gì chung. Tôi đang nghĩ đến người phụ nữ lớn tuổi điều hành tiệm bánh ở cuối một con phố tại Napoli, người trực đêm tại một khách sạn nhỏ ở Lyon, người cứu hộ đã quan sát cùng một bể bơi ở Tel Aviv suốt mười lăm mùa hè. Không ai trong số họ biết tôi. Không ai trong số họ buộc phải tử tế. Vậy mà, khi tôi bước vào, tôi thư giãn theo một cách mà tôi chưa bao giờ thực sự làm được ở những nơi sang trọng hơn.

Điều họ cùng có chung, theo tôi, là một thói quen chú ý. Họ nhìn người trước mặt, không nhìn qua vai, và họ không có vẻ vội vã ngay cả khi đang vội. Người làm bánh ở Napoli có đôi tay đã cân nắm bột từ khi cô còn là thiếu niên. Cô biết qua cái liếc mắt liệu tôi muốn một hay hai cái bánh. Người trực đêm tự tay viết số phòng lên thẻ chìa khóa, chậm rãi, trong khi nói cho tôi biết cửa sổ nào sẽ đón được ánh sáng buổi sáng. Những hành động chú ý nhỏ bé này có thể nghe nhàm chán trên giấy, nhưng đó là những thứ hệ thần kinh của ta đọc đầu tiên. Trong khi một nụ cười nhanh, lơ đãng giống như một cách phớt lờ, sự chú ý chậm rãi giống như một lời hứa nhỏ.

Một số người sẽ nói rằng tin tưởng là nguy hiểm, rằng người lạ có thể giả vờ đôi tay điềm tĩnh và ánh mắt ấm áp. Tất nhiên là họ đúng. Tôi đã bị lừa một hai lần, và tôi đã học cách giữ ví bên trong áo khoác. Tuy nhiên, tôi cho rằng phương án thay thế — nghi ngờ tất cả mọi người như nhau — còn tệ hơn việc thỉnh thoảng nhầm. Nếu tôi từ chối tin tưởng người làm bánh, người trực đêm, và người cứu hộ, thế giới của tôi sẽ thu hẹp lại thành một chiếc hộp nhỏ những người tôi đã quen. Điểm hay của một thành phố tốt, hay một ngôi làng tốt, là bằng chứng nhẹ nhàng rằng bạn có thể hạ sự đề phòng với một người bạn chưa bao giờ gặp. Phần lớn thời gian, những người lạ này xứng đáng. Vài lần họ không xứng đáng, mất mát là nhỏ. Những lần hiếm hoi mà họ có, ngày bỗng rộng hơn rất nhiều so với khi bạn vừa thức dậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'might' for a tentative argument?
options: These small acts of focus might sounded boring on paper.
These small acts of focus might sound boring on paper.
These small acts of focus mights sound boring on paper.
These small acts of focus might to sound boring on paper.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal verbs like 'might' are followed by the bare infinitive (without 's' and without 'to').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause naturally?
options: The lifeguard, which has been watching the same pool for fifteen summers, is calm.
The lifeguard who has been watching the same pool for fifteen summers is calm.
The lifeguard who he has been watching the same pool fifteen summers is calm.
The lifeguard what has been watching the same pool for fifteen summers is calm.
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for people; don't repeat the subject inside the relative clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whereas' for contrast correctly?
options: Whereas a quick, distracted smile feels like a brush-off, slow attention feels like a small promise.
A quick distracted smile feels like a brush-off whereas of slow attention feels like a promise.
Whereas as a quick smile feels like a brush-off, but slow attention feels like a promise.
A quick smile feels brush-off, whereas, slow attention feels promise.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whereas' links two contrasting clauses; it goes at the start, with a comma between them.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the natural present perfect continuous sentence.
options: I have been try to work out what they have in common.
I am trying to work out what they have in common, for a long time.
I have been trying to work out what they have in common.
I been trying to work out what they have in common.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have been + verb-ing' is the present perfect continuous, used for ongoing effort up to now.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'however' correctly as a linker?
options: However, I'd argue that the alternative is worse than being occasionally wrong.
However that the alternative I'd argue is worse than wrong.
However, that the alternative is worse, I'd argue.
However is worse than wrong, I'd argue that the alternative.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However' starts a sentence, followed by a comma, then a full clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence shows correct article use for a generic noun?
options: The point of a good city is the gentle proof that you can rest your guard.
Point of good city is gentle proof you rest your guard.
Point of a good city is gentle proof.
The point of the good city is the gentle proof that you can rest the your guard.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A good city' is non-specific (any good city); 'the gentle proof' is then specific (the one being defined).
mcQuestions.hint: First mention generic = 'a', then specific reference = 'the'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is most natural at B1 for an opinion phrase?
options: What they all share, in my view, is a habit of attention.
What they all share is, in my view of, a habit of attention.
In my view, what share they all, is a habit attention.
They all share what, in my view, is a habit attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In my view' is a clean inserted opinion phrase, between commas, in the middle of a cleft sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an opinion phrase tucked neatly between two commas.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct verb form after 'refused to'.
options: If I refused to trust the baker, my world would shrink.
If I refused to trusting the baker, my world would shrink.
If I refused trust the baker, my world would shrink.
If I refused to trusted the baker, my world would shrunk.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Refuse' is followed by 'to + base verb', and the main clause uses 'would + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct word order?
options: They look at the people in front of them, not over their shoulder.
They look in front of the people them, not over the shoulder of them.
They at the people look in front of them, not over their shoulder.
In front of them they at the people look, not over their shoulder.
mcQuestions.explanation: English word order is subject-verb-object with the prepositional phrase after the object.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'most of the time' correctly?
options: Most of the time, these strangers earn it.
The most of the time, these strangers earn it.
In the most of time, these strangers earn it.
Most of time, these strangers earn it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Most of the time' is a fixed phrase with 'the' but no article before 'most'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the fixed phrase with one 'the'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses present perfect for a small life experience?
options: I have been taken in once or twice, and I have learned to keep my wallet inside my jacket.
I been taking in once or twice, and I have learn to keep my wallet inside my jacket.
I am taken in once or twice, and I learn to keep my wallet.
I taking in once or twice, and learning to keep my wallet.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (have + past participle) expresses experience without a fixed time, paired here with a lesson learned.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have' + past participle; experience without exact time.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Can you think of a profession we used to trust on sight a generation ago, but no longer do? What changed?
